questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of looser stools and occasional blood mixed with stool. He has no weight loss. Abdominal examination is normal. Digital rectal examination is normal. Faecal occult blood test is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Change in bowel habit with rectal bleeding in an older patient is classic for colorectal cancer. Diverticular disease typically presents with left-sided pain and intermittent bleeding; IBD often occurs in younger patients; IBS does not cause blood; haemorrhoids cause bright red blood with defecation, not mixed with stool.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman is found to have iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 102 g/L, MCV 78 fL) on routine bloods. She feels tired but has no gastrointestinal symptoms. Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency anaemia without GI symptoms in a postmenopausal woman is a classic presentation of right-sided colon cancer. Coeliac disease and gastric cancer are excluded by normal OGD; angiodysplasia typically causes overt bleeding; NSAIDs cause gastritis, not isolated anaemia.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with a 2-day history of absolute constipation, abdominal distension, and vomiting. Abdomen is distended and tympanitic. Digital rectal examination shows an empty rectum. Abdominal X-ray shows dilated large bowel up to the splenic flexure with a cut-off sign. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A cut-off sign at the splenic flexure indicates a mechanical obstruction, most likely from left-sided colorectal cancer. Sigmoid volvulus shows a 'coffee bean' sign; pseudo-obstruction has uniform dilatation; faecal impaction shows a loaded rectum; diverticulitis typically has pain and tenderness.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with known diverticulosis presents with acute onset severe left iliac fossa pain, fever, and rebound tenderness. CT shows free air and a mass with surrounding inflammation. She has never had a colonoscopy. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In an elderly patient without prior colonoscopy, a perforated mass on CT is most likely colonic carcinoma. Perforated diverticulitis would have a history of diverticulosis but CT typically shows diverticula; perforated peptic ulcer is epigastric; appendicitis is right-sided; ischaemic colitis often presents with bloody diarrhoea.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with a family history of colorectal cancer (father diagnosed at age 55) presents with rectal bleeding and a feeling of incomplete evacuation. Digital rectal examination is normal. Sigmoidoscopy reveals a nodular mass at 8 cm from the anal verge. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Family history and a mass on sigmoidoscopy suggest rectal adenocarcinoma. Haemorrhoids do not form a mass; anal SCC involves the anal canal; ulcerative proctitis shows inflamed mucosa without a mass; villous adenoma is benign but less likely in this context.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of rectal bleeding and change in bowel habit towards looser stools. He has no abdominal pain or weight loss. On examination, there is no palpable mass. What is the single most appropriate first investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is the gold standard for diagnosing colorectal cancer as it allows direct visualisation and biopsy of the entire colon. Flexible sigmoidoscopy would miss right-sided lesions; CT colonography is an alternative but does not provide tissue; FIT is a screening tool, not diagnostic; barium enema is rarely used now.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman is found to have iron deficiency anaemia (Hb 105 g/L, MCV 78 fL). She is asymptomatic with no rectal bleeding. Her FIT result is positive at 120 µg Hb/g faeces. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: A positive FIT in the context of iron deficiency anaemia mandates lower GI investigation. Colonoscopy is the preferred test as it can visualise the entire colon and obtain biopsies. OGD is not first-line because the FIT points to a colonic source; CT colonography is an alternative but less sensitive for flat lesions; capsule endoscopy is not indicated for suspected colorectal cancer.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with altered bowel habit for 3 weeks. He has no rectal bleeding or family history of colorectal cancer. A faecal immunochemical test (FIT) is performed and returns a result of 25 µg Hb/g faeces. According to NICE guidelines for symptomatic patients, what is the correct interpretation of this result?
questions.explanation: NICE guideline NG12 recommends a FIT threshold of >10 µg Hb/g faeces for referral for suspected colorectal cancer in symptomatic patients. A result of 25 µg/g is positive and warrants urgent referral for colonoscopy. The other options either misinterpret the threshold or suggest unnecessary alternatives.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 4-week history of constipation and bloating. She has no rectal bleeding, weight loss, or tenesmus. Her father had colorectal cancer diagnosed at age 50. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm or exclude colorectal cancer?
options: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
questions.explanation: Colonoscopy is the diagnostic test of choice in symptomatic patients with a first-degree relative under 60 with colorectal cancer, as it allows full colonic examination and biopsy. Flexible sigmoidoscopy would miss proximal lesions; CT colonography is less sensitive for small polyps; FIT is screening, not diagnostic; CT abdomen/pelvis is not sufficient for mucosal detail.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with severe COPD (home oxygen) and heart failure (EF 35%) presents with rectal bleeding and weight loss. She is too frail for colonoscopy under sedation. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis of colorectal cancer?
questions.explanation: CT colonography (virtual colonoscopy) is the preferred alternative when colonoscopy is contraindicated or unsuitable due to frailty or comorbidities. Flexible sigmoidoscopy only examines the left colon; FIT is not diagnostic; barium enema is rarely performed and less sensitive; colonoscopy with sedation carries high risk in this patient.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old asymptomatic man is found to have a 1.5 cm Dukes' A (T1N0M0) adenocarcinoma of the ascending colon at screening colonoscopy. He has no comorbidities. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For a T1N0M0 colon cancer with favourable histology, endoscopic mucosal resection is curative and avoids the morbidity of surgery. Right hemicolectomy would be overtreatment for this early stage. Capecitabine is not indicated as adjuvant therapy for stage I disease.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with altered bowel habit and is found to have a T3N1M0 adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon. She is fit for surgery. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: For stage III colon cancer, the standard of care is surgical resection with en bloc lymph node clearance. Neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy is reserved for rectal cancer, not colon. Palliative chemotherapy would be inappropriate as the disease is potentially curable.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of absolute constipation and abdominal distension. CT shows a stenotic tumour in the descending colon causing large bowel obstruction. There is no evidence of perforation or metastasis. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Self-expanding metallic stent (SEMS) insertion as bridge to surgery
Urgent laparotomy with transverse loop colostomy
questions.explanation: For left-sided colonic obstruction, SEMS is the preferred bridge to surgery, allowing decompression and elective one-stage resection. Emergency Hartmann's or colostomy often require a stoma and have higher morbidity. Palliative stenting is for metastatic disease not amenable to resection.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with known metastatic rectal cancer (liver and lung metastases) presents with massive fresh bleeding per rectum. Her haemoglobin is 70 g/L and she is haemodynamically stable after transfusion. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment to control the bleeding?
options: Palliative short-course radiotherapy to the pelvic tumour
questions.explanation: For palliation of bleeding from an inoperable rectal cancer, short-course radiotherapy is highly effective and recommended by NICE. Surgery carries significant risk and is not first-line. Endoscopic therapy may be used but is less available and effective for mass bleeding.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man underwent a curative right hemicolectomy for a T3N0M0 adenocarcinoma of the caecum (low-risk stage II). He has no high-risk features (e.g., poor differentiation, lymphovascular invasion). What is the most appropriate follow-up strategy?
options: 5 years of annual CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and colonoscopy
3 years of CEA monitoring every 6 months and colonoscopy at 1 and 3 years
Adjuvant chemotherapy with capecitabine and oxaliplatin (CAPOX)
questions.explanation: For low-risk stage II colon cancer, NICE recommends CEA every 6 months for 3 years and colonoscopy at 1 and 3 years. CT is not routinely indicated for asymptomatic low-risk patients. Adjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended for low-risk stage II.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents to the GP with a 2-week history of rectal bleeding and change in bowel habit. He has no significant past medical history. FIT test is positive. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for urgent colonoscopy within 2 weeks
Start iron supplementation and review in 4 weeks
questions.explanation: NICE NG12 recommends urgent colonoscopy (within 2 weeks) for patients aged 60+ with rectal bleeding and change in bowel habit, or for those with a positive FIT. Flexible sigmoidoscopy is insufficient for a complete colonic assessment. CT colonography is an alternative only if colonoscopy is contraindicated. Repeat FIT or iron alone delays diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with known left-sided colonic cancer awaiting elective resection presents to the ED with abdominal distension, vomiting, and absolute constipation for 2 days. She is haemodynamically stable with no peritonism. CT confirms large bowel obstruction at the rectosigmoid. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a CT-guided biopsy of the tumour
Insert a nasogastric tube and start IV fluids
questions.explanation: In large bowel obstruction without peritonism, the immediate priority is resuscitation with NG decompression and IV fluids. Definitive management (e.g., stent or surgery) can follow once the patient is stable. Antibiotics are not indicated without signs of perforation. Stenting is a later decision.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old male with known metastatic colorectal cancer presents to the ED with severe abdominal pain, distension, and peritonism. BP 110/70 mmHg, HR 100 bpm. CT shows free air under the diaphragm. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: List the patient for emergency laparotomy
questions.explanation: With peritonism and free air (perforation), the definitive treatment is emergency laparotomy. While resuscitation (fluids, antibiotics) is important, the single most critical immediate action is to prepare for surgery to control the source of sepsis. Delaying surgery worsens outcomes.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman is 3 days post right hemicolectomy for colon cancer. She develops a fever of 38.5°C, tachycardia (HR 110 bpm), and purulent discharge from the wound. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Apply a pressure dressing to the wound
Take blood cultures and start IV broad-spectrum antibiotics
Remove wound staples and explore the wound
questions.explanation: In suspected surgical site infection with signs of sepsis, the immediate priority is to obtain blood cultures and administer antibiotics within 1 hour (NICE sepsis guidelines). Wound exploration or imaging should not delay antibiotics. Simply requesting a review is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old asymptomatic man is referred to the colorectal clinic. His mother was diagnosed with colorectal cancer at age 50. He has no bowel symptoms and no other family history. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE NG12 defines moderate risk as one first-degree relative diagnosed under 60 years. The recommended screening is a colonoscopy at age 55 (or 10 years younger than the relative's age at diagnosis). FIT is for average risk. Genetic counselling is for high-risk families (e.g., multiple affected relatives, young onset). Discharge is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with a 4-week history of constipation and tenesmus. On digital rectal examination, you feel a hard, irregular, fixed mass in the rectal ampulla. Which of the following findings is the strongest indication that this patient requires an urgent 2-week-wait referral for suspected colorectal cancer?
options: Normal stool colour on the glove
Presence of blood on the examining finger
questions.explanation: A hard, irregular, fixed mass on DRE is highly suspicious for rectal carcinoma and mandates urgent referral. Blood on the finger is non-specific and can occur with benign conditions like haemorrhoids.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman with known sigmoid colon cancer presents with absolute constipation, abdominal distension, and colicky abdominal pain for 24 hours. On examination, which of the following signs should raise concern for impending perforation?
options: Tympanic percussion over the distended abdomen
Mild rebound tenderness in the left lower quadrant
questions.explanation: In large bowel obstruction, generalised guarding is a sign of peritonitis due to perforation or ischaemia. High-pitched sounds are typical of obstruction but not an emergency sign of perforation.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of rectal adenocarcinoma presents with a large amount of bright red blood per rectum. He feels dizzy on standing. On examination, heart rate supine is 96 bpm, BP supine 120/80 mmHg. Which of the following clinical findings indicates that he has significant haemodynamic compromise requiring immediate fluid resuscitation?
options: Postural drop in systolic BP of 25 mmHg
Respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute
questions.explanation: A postural drop in systolic BP >20 mmHg is a sign of significant blood loss and haemodynamic instability, even if supine vitals are normal. It warrants immediate IV fluids and urgent assessment.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known descending colon carcinoma presents with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain. On examination, he is lying still, and his abdomen is rigid with board-like consistency. There is no bowel sounds. Which of the following findings is most consistent with a perforated viscus?
options: Rebound tenderness in the right lower quadrant
questions.explanation: Board-like rigidity is a hallmark of peritonitis due to perforation. Localised rebound is less specific. Cullen's sign is associated with retroperitoneal bleeding, not typical of colonic perforation.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with metastatic colorectal cancer receiving FOLFOX chemotherapy presents with a temperature of 38.5°C and rigors. She looks unwell. Which of the following laboratory findings is the most important trigger for immediate administration of intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics?
questions.explanation: In patients on chemotherapy, a neutrophil count <0.5 x10^9/L with fever is neutropenic sepsis, a medical emergency. Other parameters are less specific/not defining.
